name: MrBang21
content: I wasn't circumcised because I was born in a country where the practice is uncommon outside of specific religious communities.

The first time I became aware of circumcision was as a child in school. One of my classmates was circumcised, and when we asked about it, he explained that it had been done because of his family's religion. What stood out to me was that it had not been his choice. We were curious, confused, and left wondering why anyone would alter a child's body for reasons the child could neither understand nor consent to. Nobody mocked him. If anything, we felt sympathy for him.

As I grew older, I learned more about circumcision, its history, the anatomy involved, and the experiences of men who had undergone it. What surprised me most was how often the voices of circumcised men were ignored, especially when their experiences challenged popular narratives. Some described feelings of loss, anger, betrayal, or grief after discovering what had been done to them. Others spoke about physical consequences, altered sensation, complications, or simply the fact that a permanent decision had been made on their behalf.

I have also spoken with men who were circumcised later in life for medical reasons. Many of them described the experience as something they would have preferred to avoid if alternative treatments had been available. Their stories reinforced the importance of informed consent and personal choice.

Over the years, I came to realize that the debate is often dominated by doctors, religious authorities, activists, politicians, and parents, while the people most directly affected are frequently pushed to the margins. Whether someone is circumcised and content with it, regrets it, or feels deeply harmed by it, their voice deserves to be heard.
That is why I created this project.

Voices exists to document the experiences, thoughts, and feelings of people affected by circumcision. Many contributors are men who were circumcised as infants and later came to oppose the practice. Others are women who view the routine cutting of healthy children as a violation of bodily autonomy. Some share stories of loss, while others simply question why such a personal decision was taken from them.
This project is not about speaking for others. It is about giving people a place to speak for themselves.

Every testimony represents a human being, a unique life, and a perspective that deserves to be preserved. Even when people disagree, understanding begins with listening. My hope is that these stories encourage reflection, empathy, and a greater respect for every person's right to make decisions about their own body.

name: Nikorasu85
content: I complain!
I will always wish it were not done to me.
It caused years of confusion. I now know what I'm missing and I will never get to experience what an uncut man gets to.
Someone else got to decide how much sensation and pleasure I get to feel in life.

name: Fokam Kuate Hervé
content: Location: Douala, Cameroon
Male genital mutilation (circumcision) is legal here. Female genital mutilation is illegal.
I did not realize I had been circumcised until I was 30 years old. Discovering that a part of my body had been permanently removed without my knowledge or consent was shocking.

When I confronted my parents, they gave me every justification imaginable. Tradition. Religion. HIV prevention. Claims that women prefer it. Even accusations that I had been "brainwashed" by France. Excuse after excuse, anything except acknowledging what had been done to me.
I demanded one of two things: a sincere apology or that I would remove them from my life. They knelt and asked for forgiveness, and for a time I believed they genuinely regretted their decision.
Later, I realized nothing had really changed.

Whenever I tried to discuss circumcision, they ignored me. They allowed their financially dependent son to have their grandchildren circumcised. They continued associating with relatives who tried to silence me whenever I spoke about the issue.
Their actions showed me that they did not truly regret what they had done. They were comfortable maintaining relationships with people who supported and perpetuated the same practice.
The hardest part was not discovering that I had been circumcised. The hardest part was realizing how little concern the people around me had for what had been taken from me.
That realization changed how I viewed my family. Eventually, I chose to cut ties and disappear from their lives. Today, none of my relatives know where I live.

My experience taught me that circumcision is not only about the physical act itself. It is also about the refusal to listen to those who object to what was done to them, even after they find the courage to speak.

name: Anonymous Male from America
content: I was circumsized when I was an infant. I learned about the functions of the foreskin when I was a teenager and confronted my father about his decision to mutilate my body. He brushed it off and said it was done to him as well. This is ritualistic abuse done to so many children for no legitimate reason. I don’t care about someone‘s religion. If your religion compells you to do this to infants or any man then you‘re a monster. If you have this done to your children because it was done to you then you are a coward. I have been restoring my foreskin, and it is a long, tiring process. I want everyone responsible for this practice to hang. I want to see a world where circumsision is eradicated.
